skills.skill: Atomic Structure and the Periodic Table
skills.teks_description: Explain the Periodic Table's development over time using chemical and physical properties evidence
skills.prompt: This topic covers atomic structure and the Periodic Table for Chemistry students (TEKS C.5(A)). Students should understand how the periodic table was developed using patterns in chemical and physical properties. Atomic structure: protons (positive, in nucleus, determine element identity), neutrons (neutral, in nucleus, determine isotope), electrons (negative, in electron cloud, determine chemical behavior). Electron configuration: Aufbau principle, Pauli exclusion principle, Hund's rule; notation using orbital diagrams and electron configurations (1s2 2s2 2p6...). Periodic trends: atomic radius (decreases across period, increases down group), ionization energy (increases across, decreases down), electronegativity (increases across, decreases down), electron affinity. Groups: alkali metals (1), alkaline earth metals (2), halogens (17), noble gases (18), transition metals. Key concepts: valence electrons determine chemical properties, metals vs. nonmetals vs. metalloids, Mendeleev's predictions. Vocabulary: atomic number, mass number, isotope, ion, orbital, sublevel, period, group, valence electron, core electron. Common misconceptions: electrons orbit like planets; the periodic table is just for memorization.

skills.teks_description: Construct arguments supporting periodic trend predictions of bonding between elements
skills.prompt: This topic covers chemical bonding for Chemistry students (TEKS C.7(A)). Students should understand how atoms bond based on periodic trends and electron configurations. Types of bonds: ionic (transfer of electrons between metals and nonmetals, held by electrostatic attraction), covalent (sharing of electrons between nonmetals, can be single, double, or triple bonds), metallic (sea of delocalized electrons among metal atoms). Key concepts: octet rule (atoms tend to gain, lose, or share electrons to achieve 8 valence electrons), Lewis dot structures show valence electrons and bonding, electronegativity differences determine bond type (nonpolar covalent <0.5, polar covalent 0.5-1.7, ionic >1.7). Bond properties: bond length, bond energy, polarity. Intermolecular forces: London dispersion (all molecules), dipole-dipole (polar molecules), hydrogen bonding (H bonded to F, O, or N). Vocabulary: ionic bond, covalent bond, metallic bond, electronegativity, polarity, Lewis structure, lone pair, bonding pair, lattice energy, crystal lattice. Common misconceptions: ionic compounds form molecules; all covalent bonds share electrons equally.

skills.teks_description: Name and write chemical formulas for ionic and covalent compounds using IUPAC nomenclature rules
skills.prompt: This topic covers nomenclature and chemical formulas for Chemistry students (TEKS C.7(B)). Students should master IUPAC naming rules for ionic and covalent compounds. Ionic compounds: name the cation (metal) first, then anion (nonmetal with -ide suffix); for transition metals, use Roman numerals to indicate charge (e.g., Iron(III) chloride = FeCl3). Polyatomic ions: memorize common ions (sulfate SO4^2-, nitrate NO3^-, phosphate PO4^3-, carbonate CO3^2-, hydroxide OH^-, ammonium NH4^+). Covalent (molecular) compounds: use Greek prefixes (mono-, di-, tri-, tetra-, penta-, hexa-) for both elements, second element gets -ide suffix (e.g., dinitrogen pentoxide = N2O5). Writing formulas: use charge-crossing method for ionic compounds, ensure formulas are reduced to lowest whole-number ratios. Acids: binary acids (hydro- prefix, -ic suffix: HCl = hydrochloric acid), oxyacids (-ate polyatomic ion becomes -ic acid, -ite becomes -ous acid). Vocabulary: cation, anion, polyatomic ion, binary compound, formula unit, molecular formula, empirical formula, subscript, coefficient. Common misconceptions: all compounds with metals are ionic; the order of elements in a formula does not matter.

skills.teks_description: Interpret, write, and balance equations including synthesis, decomposition, single/double replacement, and combustion
skills.prompt: This topic covers chemical reactions and equations for Chemistry students (TEKS C.9(A)). Students should be able to interpret, write, and balance chemical equations. Five main reaction types: synthesis/combination (A + B → AB), decomposition (AB → A + B), single replacement (A + BC → AC + B, using activity series), double replacement (AB + CD → AD + CB, driven by precipitate, gas, or water formation), combustion (hydrocarbon + O2 → CO2 + H2O). Balancing equations: apply law of conservation of mass (atoms in = atoms out), adjust coefficients (never subscripts), balance metals first, then nonmetals, then hydrogen and oxygen last. Key concepts: identifying reaction types from equations, predicting products, using the activity series for single replacement reactions, solubility rules for double replacement. Vocabulary: reactant, product, coefficient, subscript, yield, catalyst, activity series, precipitate, spectator ion, net ionic equation, combustion, exothermic, endothermic. Common misconceptions: chemical equations show individual molecules reacting; changing subscripts is acceptable when balancing.

skills.teks_description: Perform stoichiometric calculations for mass relationships, gas volumes, and percent yield
skills.prompt: This topic covers stoichiometry for Chemistry students (TEKS C.9(C)). Students should perform calculations relating masses, moles, and volumes in chemical reactions. Core concept: mole ratios from balanced equations allow conversion between amounts of reactants and products. Problem-solving steps: (1) write and balance the equation, (2) convert given quantity to moles, (3) use mole ratio to find moles of desired substance, (4) convert to desired units. Key calculations: mole-to-mole conversions, mass-to-mass conversions, limiting reagent problems (the reactant that runs out first determines theoretical yield), percent yield = (actual yield / theoretical yield) x 100%. Gas stoichiometry: at STP, 1 mol of any gas occupies 22.4 L. Molar mass connects grams to moles. Avogadro's number (6.022 x 10^23) connects moles to particles. Vocabulary: stoichiometry, mole ratio, limiting reagent, excess reagent, theoretical yield, actual yield, percent yield, molar mass, STP, Avogadro's number. Common misconceptions: the larger amount of reactant is always the limiting reagent; coefficients represent grams.

skills.skill: States of Matter and Phase Changes
skills.teks_description: Describe kinetic molecular theory postulates relating to states of matter
skills.prompt: This topic covers states of matter and phase changes for Chemistry students (TEKS C.10(A)). Students should understand the kinetic molecular theory (KMT) and how it explains the behavior of matter in different states. KMT postulates: particles are in constant random motion, no intermolecular forces in ideal gases, kinetic energy is proportional to temperature, elastic collisions. States of matter: solid (fixed shape and volume, particles vibrate in place), liquid (fixed volume, variable shape, particles slide past each other), gas (variable shape and volume, particles move freely). Phase changes: melting (solid→liquid), freezing (liquid→solid), vaporization/boiling (liquid→gas), condensation (gas→liquid), sublimation (solid→gas), deposition (gas→solid). Key concepts: heating curves show temperature vs. energy added, plateaus during phase changes (energy changes potential energy not kinetic), heat of fusion and heat of vaporization. Vocabulary: kinetic energy, temperature, pressure, phase diagram, triple point, critical point, vapor pressure, boiling point, melting point, heat of fusion, heat of vaporization. Common misconceptions: boiling point is always 100C (it depends on pressure); bubbles in boiling water are hydrogen and oxygen.

skills.teks_description: Investigate how solid and gas solubilities are influenced by temperature using solubility curves
skills.prompt: This topic covers solutions and solubility for Chemistry students (TEKS C.11(C)). Students should understand how solutions form and factors affecting solubility. Solution components: solute (dissolved substance) and solvent (dissolving medium, usually water). Types of solutions: unsaturated (can dissolve more), saturated (maximum solute dissolved at given temperature), supersaturated (contains more than normally possible, unstable). Factors affecting solubility: temperature (most solids become more soluble as temperature increases; gases become less soluble), pressure (affects gas solubility per Henry's law), nature of solute and solvent (like dissolves like: polar dissolves polar, nonpolar dissolves nonpolar). Solubility curves: graphs showing grams of solute per 100g water vs. temperature; use to determine saturation status and amount that crystallizes upon cooling. Concentration units: molarity (M = mol solute / L solution), percent composition, parts per million (ppm). Vocabulary: solute, solvent, solubility, saturated, unsaturated, supersaturated, miscible, immiscible, dilution, concentration, molarity. Common misconceptions: all substances become more soluble at higher temperatures; dissolving is a chemical change.

skills.teks_description: Define pH and calculate solution pH using hydrogen ion concentration
skills.prompt: This topic covers acids, bases, and pH for Chemistry students (TEKS C.12(E)). Students should understand acid-base chemistry and pH calculations. Definitions: Arrhenius (acids produce H+ in water, bases produce OH-), Bronsted-Lowry (acids donate protons, bases accept protons). pH scale: ranges from 0-14; pH = -log[H+]; pH < 7 is acidic, pH = 7 is neutral, pH > 7 is basic. pOH = -log[OH-]; pH + pOH = 14. Key concepts: strong acids completely dissociate (HCl, H2SO4, HNO3), weak acids partially dissociate (CH3COOH); strong bases completely dissociate (NaOH, KOH), weak bases partially dissociate (NH3). Neutralization: acid + base → salt + water. Each pH unit represents a tenfold change in H+ concentration. Indicators change color at specific pH ranges. Buffer solutions resist pH changes. Vocabulary: acid, base, conjugate acid, conjugate base, amphoteric, neutralization, titration, equivalence point, indicator, buffer, hydronium ion, hydroxide ion, dissociation. Common misconceptions: pH 0 means no acid; all acids are dangerous; neutral pH always equals 7 (only in pure water at 25C).

skills.teks_description: Perform calculations involving heat, mass, temperature change, and specific heat
skills.prompt: This topic covers thermochemistry for Chemistry students (TEKS C.13(D)). Students should understand energy changes in chemical reactions and perform calorimetry calculations. Key equation: q = mcΔT where q = heat energy (joules), m = mass (grams), c = specific heat capacity (J/g·°C), ΔT = temperature change (Tf - Ti). Specific heat of water: 4.184 J/g·°C. Exothermic reactions release heat (ΔH negative, temperature of surroundings increases); endothermic reactions absorb heat (ΔH positive, temperature of surroundings decreases). Key concepts: enthalpy (H) is heat content at constant pressure, Hess's law (enthalpy changes are additive for stepwise reactions), standard enthalpy of formation, calorimetry measures heat transfer. Energy diagrams show activation energy, reactant and product energy levels, and effect of catalysts (lower activation energy). Vocabulary: enthalpy, exothermic, endothermic, calorimeter, specific heat, heat capacity, Hess's law, activation energy, enthalpy of formation, thermochemical equation, joule, calorie. Common misconceptions: exothermic means explosive; temperature and heat are the same thing.

skills.teks_description: Differentiate among acid-base, precipitation, and oxidation-reduction reactions
skills.prompt: This topic covers reaction rates and chemical equilibrium for Chemistry students (TEKS C.9(B)). Students should understand factors affecting reaction rates and equilibrium concepts. Reaction rates: measure how fast reactants are consumed or products formed. Factors affecting rate: concentration (higher = faster), temperature (higher = faster, more kinetic energy), surface area (greater = faster), catalysts (lower activation energy), nature of reactants. Collision theory: reactions occur when particles collide with sufficient energy (activation energy) and proper orientation. Chemical equilibrium: dynamic state where forward and reverse reaction rates are equal; concentrations remain constant but reactions continue. Le Chatelier's principle: when a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to counteract the change (adding reactant shifts right, removing product shifts right, increasing temperature shifts toward endothermic direction, pressure changes affect gas-phase equilibria). Equilibrium constant Keq = [products]/[reactants]. Vocabulary: reaction rate, collision theory, activation energy, catalyst, equilibrium, reversible reaction, Le Chatelier's principle, equilibrium constant, shift. Common misconceptions: equilibrium means equal concentrations of reactants and products; catalysts shift equilibrium.

skills.teks_description: Differentiate among acid-base, precipitation, and oxidation-reduction reactions
skills.prompt: This topic covers oxidation-reduction (redox) reactions for Chemistry students (TEKS C.9(B)). Students should understand electron transfer in chemical reactions. Key definitions: oxidation is loss of electrons (OIL), reduction is gain of electrons (RIG); together: OIL RIG. Oxidation numbers (states): rules for assigning (free elements = 0, monatomic ions = charge, O usually -2, H usually +1, sum of oxidation numbers = charge of species). Identifying redox: if oxidation numbers change, it is a redox reaction. Reducing agent: substance that is oxidized (gives electrons); oxidizing agent: substance that is reduced (accepts electrons). Types of redox reactions: combustion, single replacement, synthesis of metal oxides, electrochemical cells. Activity series: ranks metals by reactivity; more active metals can displace less active metals from solutions. Vocabulary: oxidation, reduction, oxidizing agent, reducing agent, oxidation number, half-reaction, electron transfer, activity series, redox, electrochemical cell. Common misconceptions: oxidation always involves oxygen; reduction means something gets smaller.

skills.teks_description: Explain the Periodic Table including carbon family properties and organic chemistry foundations
skills.prompt: This topic covers organic chemistry basics for Chemistry students (TEKS C.5(A)). Students should understand carbon's unique bonding properties and basic organic compounds. Carbon: has 4 valence electrons, can form 4 covalent bonds, can bond to itself creating chains, rings, and branched structures; this versatility explains the enormous diversity of organic compounds. Hydrocarbons: compounds of only carbon and hydrogen. Alkanes (single bonds, CnH2n+2, saturated), alkenes (at least one double bond, CnH2n, unsaturated), alkynes (at least one triple bond, CnH2n-2, unsaturated), aromatic (benzene ring, C6H6). Isomers: same molecular formula, different structural arrangement (structural isomers). Key properties: nonpolar (insoluble in water, soluble in organic solvents), boiling points increase with molecular size, London dispersion forces are primary intermolecular forces. Naming: IUPAC system uses root name for longest carbon chain (meth-, eth-, prop-, but-, pent-), suffix indicates bond type (-ane, -ene, -yne). Vocabulary: organic, hydrocarbon, saturated, unsaturated, isomer, functional group, polymer, monomer, IUPAC nomenclature. Common misconceptions: organic means natural or healthy; only living things produce organic compounds.

skills.teks_description: Analyze ionic, covalent, and metallic substance properties regarding intramolecular and intermolecular forces
skills.prompt: This topic covers hydrocarbons and functional groups for Chemistry students (TEKS C.7(D)). Students should understand how functional groups determine the properties of organic molecules. Major functional groups: hydroxyl (-OH, alcohols: polar, hydrogen bonding, higher boiling points), carbonyl (C=O, aldehydes and ketones), carboxyl (-COOH, carboxylic acids: weak acids, found in vinegar and amino acids), amino (-NH2, amines: basic, found in amino acids), ester (-COO-, formed from acid + alcohol, fragrances and flavors), ether (-O-, relatively unreactive), phosphate (-PO4, found in DNA and ATP). Intramolecular forces: covalent bonds within molecules determine structure. Intermolecular forces: determine physical properties; hydrogen bonding (alcohols, carboxylic acids), dipole-dipole (polar groups), London dispersion (all molecules, stronger with larger size). Key concepts: functional groups determine reactivity and physical properties, polymers are large molecules formed from repeating monomers (addition and condensation polymerization). Vocabulary: functional group, alcohol, aldehyde, ketone, carboxylic acid, ester, amine, ether, polymer, monomer, intermolecular force, intramolecular force. Common misconceptions: all organic molecules have the same properties; functional groups do not affect physical properties.

skills.teks_description: Describe alpha, beta, and gamma radioactive decay characteristics using balanced nuclear equations
skills.prompt: This topic covers nuclear chemistry for Chemistry students (TEKS C.14(A)). Students should understand types of radioactive decay and nuclear reactions. Types of decay: alpha decay (emits helium nucleus, mass decreases by 4, atomic number by 2), beta decay (neutron converts to proton, emits electron, atomic number increases by 1), gamma decay (emits high-energy photon, no change in mass or atomic number). Balancing nuclear equations: conserve mass number (top) and atomic number (bottom). Half-life: time for half of a radioactive sample to decay; calculate remaining amount using N = N0 × (1/2)^(t/t½). Nuclear fission: heavy nucleus splits into smaller nuclei, releases large energy (nuclear power plants, atomic bombs). Nuclear fusion: light nuclei combine to form heavier nucleus, releases even more energy (stars, sun). Key concepts: band of stability, nuclear binding energy, E = mc², applications (medical imaging, cancer treatment, carbon dating, nuclear power, smoke detectors). Vocabulary: radioactive decay, isotope, radioisotope, half-life, fission, fusion, chain reaction, critical mass, transmutation, nuclear equation. Common misconceptions: all radiation is harmful; nuclear reactions and chemical reactions are the same process.

skills.teks_description: Describe and calculate relationships among volume, pressure, moles, and temperature for ideal gases
skills.prompt: This topic covers gas laws for Chemistry students (TEKS C.10(B)). Students should understand and apply mathematical relationships among pressure, volume, temperature, and moles of gases. Individual gas laws: Boyle's law (P1V1 = P2V2, inverse relationship at constant T and n), Charles's law (V1/T1 = V2/T2, direct relationship at constant P and n), Gay-Lussac's law (P1/T1 = P2/T2, direct at constant V and n), Avogadro's law (V1/n1 = V2/n2, direct at constant T and P). Combined gas law: P1V1/T1 = P2V2/T2. Ideal gas law: PV = nRT where R = 0.0821 L·atm/mol·K or 8.314 J/mol·K. Dalton's law of partial pressures: Ptotal = P1 + P2 + P3... Temperature must be in Kelvin (K = °C + 273). STP: 0°C (273 K) and 1 atm; molar volume = 22.4 L/mol. Key concepts: ideal vs. real gases (real gases deviate at high pressure and low temperature). Vocabulary: pressure, volume, temperature, moles, STP, ideal gas, real gas, partial pressure, molar volume, Kelvin, atmosphere, torr, pascal. Common misconceptions: gas particles are stationary; temperature can be used in Celsius for gas law calculations.

skills.teks_description: Define mole and apply molar mass concepts to convert between moles and grams
skills.prompt: This topic covers the mole concept for Chemistry students (TEKS C.8(A)). Students should understand the mole as the fundamental counting unit in chemistry and perform conversions between moles, grams, and particles. One mole = 6.022 × 10^23 particles (Avogadro's number), equivalent to the number of atoms in exactly 12 grams of carbon-12. Molar mass: the mass of one mole of a substance in grams, numerically equal to the atomic/molecular mass in amu from the periodic table. Key conversions: grams to moles (divide by molar mass), moles to grams (multiply by molar mass), moles to particles (multiply by Avogadro's number), particles to moles (divide by Avogadro's number). Molar mass of compounds: sum of all atomic masses (e.g., H2O = 2(1.008) + 16.00 = 18.016 g/mol). Percent composition: (mass of element in 1 mol compound / molar mass of compound) × 100%. Empirical formula: simplest whole-number ratio of atoms; molecular formula: actual number of atoms. Vocabulary: mole, molar mass, Avogadro's number, formula mass, empirical formula, molecular formula, percent composition. Common misconceptions: a mole is a mass (it is a count); molar mass and atomic mass are the same thing.

skills.teks_description: Interpret, write, and balance equations including synthesis, decomposition, single replacement, double replacement, and combustion
skills.prompt: This topic covers types of chemical reactions for Chemistry students (TEKS C.9(A)). Students should identify, classify, and predict products for the five main reaction types. Synthesis (combination): two or more substances combine to form one product (A + B → AB; e.g., 2Na + Cl2 → 2NaCl). Decomposition: one compound breaks into two or more simpler substances (AB → A + B; e.g., 2H2O → 2H2 + O2). Single replacement: one element replaces another in a compound (A + BC → AC + B; use activity series to predict if reaction occurs; e.g., Zn + CuSO4 → ZnSO4 + Cu). Double replacement: ions in two compounds exchange partners (AB + CD → AD + CB; driven by formation of precipitate, gas, or water; e.g., NaCl + AgNO3 → AgCl + NaNO3). Combustion: substance reacts with O2, producing CO2 and H2O for hydrocarbons (CxHy + O2 → CO2 + H2O). Key concepts: conservation of mass, balancing equations, predicting products, net ionic equations. Vocabulary: synthesis, decomposition, single replacement, double replacement, combustion, activity series, precipitate, spectator ion. Common misconceptions: all single replacement reactions occur spontaneously; all reactions fit neatly into five categories.

skills.teks_description: Describe water's unique role in solutions regarding polarity
skills.prompt: This topic covers the unique properties of water for Chemistry students (TEKS C.11(A)). Students should understand how water's molecular structure leads to its exceptional properties. Water molecule: bent shape (104.5° bond angle), polar covalent O-H bonds, oxygen is more electronegative creating partial charges (δ- on O, δ+ on H). Hydrogen bonding: strong intermolecular force between water molecules; each water molecule can form up to 4 hydrogen bonds. Properties resulting from polarity and hydrogen bonding: high specific heat capacity (4.184 J/g·°C, resists temperature change, moderates climate), high heat of vaporization (cooling effect of evaporation), surface tension (cohesion creates water droplets, insects walking on water), capillary action (adhesion and cohesion allow water to climb narrow tubes), universal solvent (dissolves ionic and polar substances), ice is less dense than liquid water (hydrogen bonds form crystal lattice, ice floats, insulates aquatic ecosystems). Vocabulary: polarity, hydrogen bond, cohesion, adhesion, surface tension, capillary action, specific heat, heat of vaporization, universal solvent, hydrophobic, hydrophilic. Common misconceptions: water is the only polar molecule; ice sinks in water.

skills.teks_description: Classify processes as exothermic or endothermic and represent energy changes using thermochemical equations
skills.prompt: This topic covers chemical kinetics and energy changes for Chemistry students (TEKS C.13(C)). Students should classify reactions by energy changes and understand reaction energetics. Exothermic processes: release energy to surroundings, ΔH < 0, products have lower energy than reactants (e.g., combustion, neutralization, most crystallization). Endothermic processes: absorb energy from surroundings, ΔH > 0, products have higher energy than reactants (e.g., photosynthesis, melting ice, dissolving ammonium nitrate). Thermochemical equations: balanced equations including enthalpy change (ΔH value). Energy diagrams: plot potential energy vs. reaction progress, showing reactant energy, product energy, activation energy (Ea), and transition state. Catalysts lower activation energy without being consumed; they do not change ΔH. Key concepts: system vs. surroundings, law of conservation of energy (first law of thermodynamics), enthalpy vs. entropy, spontaneity (Gibbs free energy ΔG = ΔH - TΔS). Vocabulary: exothermic, endothermic, enthalpy, entropy, activation energy, catalyst, transition state, thermochemical equation, Gibbs free energy, spontaneous, nonspontaneous. Common misconceptions: endothermic reactions cannot be spontaneous; catalysts provide energy for the reaction.

skills.teks_description: Differentiate among acid-base, precipitation, and oxidation-reduction reactions including electrochemical processes
skills.prompt: This topic covers electrochemistry for Chemistry students (TEKS C.9(B)). Students should understand the relationship between chemical reactions and electrical energy. Electrochemistry links redox reactions to electricity. Galvanic (voltaic) cells: spontaneous redox reactions produce electrical energy; anode (oxidation occurs, negative terminal), cathode (reduction occurs, positive terminal), salt bridge maintains electrical neutrality, electron flow from anode to cathode through external circuit. Electrolytic cells: nonspontaneous reactions driven by external electrical energy (electrolysis); used for electroplating, purifying metals, decomposing compounds. Standard reduction potentials (E°): measure tendency to gain electrons; more positive = stronger oxidizing agent; cell potential E°cell = E°cathode - E°anode; positive E°cell means spontaneous. Key concepts: half-reactions, balancing redox equations, Faraday's laws of electrolysis, applications (batteries, fuel cells, corrosion prevention, electroplating). Vocabulary: galvanic cell, electrolytic cell, anode, cathode, salt bridge, half-reaction, cell potential, standard reduction potential, electrolysis, electroplating, corrosion. Common misconceptions: electrons flow through the salt bridge; electrolytic and galvanic cells work the same way.
